_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Sanders et al. Am J Sports Med 2016 (PMID 27261443): 68.6 per 100,000 person-years US. KANON trial (Frobell et al. NEJM 2010, PMID 20660401) for ACL-reconstruction-vs-rehab. AAOS 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline. STaR trial 2022 (NEJM 386(15):1383-1395). Return-to-sport criteria from Grindem et al. BJSM 2016. MRI sensitivity from Crawford et al. 2007.

h1Title: ACL Tear: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment
heroOverview: An anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tear is a partial or complete rupture of the central knee ligament that stabilizes the tibia against forward translation and rotation on the femur. ACL injury is the most common ligamentous knee injury in athletes, with an annual US incidence of approximately 69 per 100,000 person-years and 200,000-250,000 cases each year, with peak risk between ages 15 and 25. Most tears occur during non-contact pivoting, cutting, or landing in football, basketball, skiing, soccer, and netball, accompanied by an audible pop, rapid swelling within hours, and inability to continue activity. Untreated complete ruptures lead to recurrent instability, meniscal damage, and accelerated post-traumatic osteoarthritis. Modern management combines structured rehabilitation with or without arthroscopic reconstruction, with 80-90% of athletes returning to sport at 9-12 months.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 69 per 100,000 person-years in the US (Sanders et al. 2016); rising in adolescent female athletes
keyStats.demographics: Female athletes 2-8× higher risk than males in same sports; peak incidence ages 15-25; 50-70% involve non-contact mechanisms
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at injury 25-30 years; second peak in skiing-related injuries ages 40-55
keyStats.globalCases: 200,000-250,000 ACL injuries annually in the United States; equivalent rates in other high-income countries; roughly 100,000-130,000 ACL reconstructions performed each year in the US

definition: An ACL tear (ICD-10: S83.5) is partial or complete disruption of the anterior cruciate ligament, the dominant restraint to anterior tibial translation and rotational instability of the knee. The ACL is an intra-articular ligament running from the lateral femoral condyle to the anterior tibial spine, with two functional bundles (anteromedial and posterolateral). Most injuries are non-contact, occurring during sudden deceleration, sidestep cutting, pivoting on a planted foot, or landing from a jump with the knee in valgus, internal rotation, and near-extension. The mechanism produces a characteristic sequence: audible pop, immediate giving-way, large haemarthrosis within 4-12 hours, severe pain, and inability to bear weight. Concomitant injuries are common: meniscal tears in 50-70%, articular cartilage lesions in 20-30%, bone bruises on MRI in 80%, medial collateral ligament injury in 25%, and the so-called unhappy triad (ACL, MCL, medial meniscus) in 5-10%. Without functional treatment, complete tears leave the knee unstable for cutting and pivoting activities, leading to recurrent giving-way episodes, secondary meniscal injury, and accelerated post-traumatic osteoarthritis (45-70% by 10-20 years). The ACL has poor intrinsic healing capacity and primary repair is rarely successful; reconstruction with autograft (patellar tendon, hamstring, quadriceps tendon) or allograft remains standard. Sports medicine, orthopaedic surgeons, physiotherapists, and athletic trainers manage the condition.

typesClassification.description: The most common pattern (>80% of complete tears). Mid-substance fibre disruption with no continuity. Diagnosed by positive Lachman test (grade 2-3), positive pivot shift, and MRI showing complete fibre discontinuity. Almost always requires functional bracing, rehabilitation, or reconstruction for return to pivoting sport.

Disruption of one bundle (anteromedial or posterolateral) with intact fibres in the other. Clinical examination shows minor instability; MRI may underestimate. Management is rehabilitation-led with monitoring; selected patients with rotational instability proceed to reconstruction.

typesClassification.type: Bony avulsion ACL tear (tibial spine avulsion)
typesClassification.description: More common in skeletally immature patients; the ACL pulls off a fragment of the tibial intercondylar eminence. Treatment is open or arthroscopic fixation of the bony fragment rather than ligament reconstruction.

typesClassification.type: ACL tear with multi-ligament knee injury
typesClassification.description: Combined injury to two or more knee ligaments (PCL, collaterals, posterolateral corner) — often after high-energy trauma or knee dislocation. Requires staged surgical reconstruction and rules out vascular injury (popliteal artery).

ACL graft failure after primary reconstruction occurs in 3-15% (higher in young female athletes returning to pivoting sport). Revision reconstruction is more complex and may require staged tunnel grafting.

primarySymptoms: An audible pop or popping sensation in the knee at the moment of injury, reported by 50-70% of patients.
Immediate sense of the knee 'giving way' or buckling, with inability to continue the activity.
Rapid joint swelling within 4-12 hours from haemarthrosis (bleeding into the joint).
Severe pain at the moment of injury that often subsides somewhat within hours but worsens with weight bearing.
Loss of full knee extension and reduced flexion from swelling and pain.
Limp or inability to bear full weight in the acute phase.
Persistent giving-way episodes (the knee buckling on pivoting or stair descent) in untreated complete tears.
Sensation of instability when changing direction, twisting, or descending slopes.
Knee pain along the joint line or in the medial compartment from associated meniscal injury.
Quadriceps muscle weakness and atrophy within 2-4 weeks of injury from disuse and reflex inhibition.
earlyWarningSigns: An audible pop in the knee during pivoting, cutting, jumping, or landing
Rapid swelling of the knee within hours of injury rather than days
Inability to continue play after a non-contact knee injury
A sense of the knee being 'loose' or 'unstable' that persists after initial swelling resolves
Loss of full knee straightening (extension deficit) in the days following injury
Recurrent giving-way episodes during ordinary activities after an apparent minor knee injury
emergencySigns: Knee dislocation with gross deformity — emergency reduction and vascular assessment to exclude popliteal artery injury
Absent or diminished foot pulses, cool limb, or pale skin after knee injury — urgent vascular surgical referral
Numbness, foot drop, or motor weakness in the leg suggesting common peroneal nerve injury
Open knee injury with visible joint capsule or bone — surgical emergency
Compartment syndrome with severe out-of-proportion pain, tense calf, and paraesthesia
High-energy knee injury with multiple ligament involvement (knee dislocation) — exclude vascular and neurological injury

causes.description: Up to 70% of ACL tears occur without direct contact. A sudden change of direction with the foot planted, knee flexed and rotated inward, generates rotational and valgus forces that exceed the ACL's tensile strength. Common in football, basketball, soccer, netball, and skiing.

Landing from a jump with the knee in dynamic valgus collapse, with the hip in adduction and internal rotation and the trunk leaning, places extreme load on the ACL. The mechanism is responsible for many basketball, volleyball, and gymnastics injuries.

Sudden braking from a sprint or hyperextension of the knee (e.g., a ball striking the front of the leg, sliding tackle) can rupture the ACL directly. Common in football, hurdling, and certain dance moves.

A direct blow to the lateral knee in football, rugby, or martial arts can produce the unhappy triad of ACL, MCL, and medial meniscus injury. Less common than non-contact mechanisms (about 30%) but typically more severe.

Narrow intercondylar notch, increased posterior tibial slope, wide pelvis and Q-angle, smaller ACL cross-section, and joint laxity all increase the risk. Female anatomy contributes to the 2-8× higher rates seen in women playing the same sports.

Quadriceps-dominant landing patterns, weak hip abductors, reduced hamstring co-contraction, and limited core stability raise dynamic valgus and ACL load. Neuromuscular training programmes (PEP, FIFA 11+) reduce non-contact ACL injuries by 40-70% in adolescent athletes.

riskFactors.description: Women have 2-8× higher ACL injury rates than men in the same sports, particularly soccer, basketball, and team handball. Hormonal, anatomical, and neuromuscular factors contribute.
Incidence peaks between ages 15 and 25 in both sexes; the highest sport-specific risk is in adolescent female soccer and basketball players.
Football, soccer, basketball, netball, rugby, skiing, and team handball carry the highest ACL injury rates. Exposure (hours played) is a strong determinant.
Patients with a previous ACL reconstruction have approximately 6× increased risk of contralateral ACL tear and 3-15% graft failure rate, especially in young female athletes.

riskFactors.factor: Narrow intercondylar notch or posterior tibial slope > 12°
riskFactors.description: Anatomical factors raising ACL strain during knee motion. Posterior tibial slope is increasingly recognized as a modifiable risk in revision settings via slope-reducing osteotomy.

riskFactors.factor: Poor neuromuscular control and dynamic valgus
riskFactors.description: Knee valgus collapse on landing is the strongest biomechanical predictor of non-contact ACL injury. Targeted neuromuscular training reduces risk by 40-70%.

riskFactors.factor: Inadequate physical preparation and high training load
riskFactors.description: Sudden increases in training load, especially after injury or off-season, raise ACL injury risk. Periodised training and gradual return-to-sport progressions reduce risk.

affectedDemographics: Adolescent and young adult athletes (ages 15-25) in pivoting sports carry the highest absolute risk.
Female athletes in soccer, basketball, netball, and team handball have 2-8× higher rates than male peers.
Skiers aged 40-55 form a second peak, with mostly women affected.
Patients with previous ACL reconstruction: 6-15% sustain contralateral or ipsilateral graft injury within 5 years.
Recreational athletes have lower per-hour but higher overall absolute injury rates because of larger population.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines mechanism of injury, clinical examination, and confirmatory imaging. The history of an audible pop, immediate giving-way, and rapid effusion in a young athlete is highly suggestive. Examination begins with inspection (swelling, bruising, deformity), palpation (joint line tenderness, effusion), assessment of range of motion, and ligamentous testing. The Lachman test (knee flexed 20-30°, anterior pull on the tibia) is the most sensitive bedside test (sensitivity 80-95%, specificity 90-95%); a soft endpoint and increased anterior tibial translation compared with the contralateral knee indicate ACL injury. The anterior drawer test (knee flexed 90°) is less sensitive in the acute setting. The pivot shift test (combined valgus, internal rotation, and flexion) is highly specific when positive but often difficult in conscious patients. KT-1000 arthrometer and Telos stress radiography quantify side-to-side anterior translation in research and pre-operative settings. McMurray and Apley tests assess meniscal injury. MRI is the gold standard imaging investigation, with sensitivity 86-100% and specificity 95-100% for complete tears; characteristic findings include disrupted ACL fibres, increased ACL signal, abnormal orientation, lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibial bone bruises, and associated meniscal or chondral injury. Plain radiographs are obtained to exclude fracture (Segond fracture — a small avulsion of the lateral tibia — is pathognomonic for ACL injury). Diagnostic arthroscopy is reserved for cases where examination and MRI are non-diagnostic, or as part of surgical reconstruction. Differential diagnosis includes PCL injury, posterolateral corner injury, meniscal tear without ACL injury, patellar dislocation, and tibial plateau fracture.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Most sensitive bedside test for ACL injury; assesses anterior tibial translation at 20-30° flexion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Examiner stabilizes the femur and pulls the tibia forward; takes seconds. Increased translation or absent endpoint compared with the opposite knee indicates ACL injury.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reproduces dynamic rotatory instability characteristic of complete ACL rupture
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed with the knee extended and tibia internally rotated, applying valgus stress while flexing the knee. A 'clunk' as the knee subluxes and reduces is positive. May be impossible to elicit in awake patients with guarding.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects anterior tibial translation with knee flexed 90°
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient supine with knee at 90°; examiner pulls tibia forward. Less sensitive than Lachman in the acute setting; helpful in chronic cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard imaging for ACL tear; identifies associated meniscal, chondral, and ligamentous injuries
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20-30 minutes lying still in the scanner. Open MRI available for claustrophobic patients. Results within 24-48 hours; high sensitivity and specificity for complete tear.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes fracture (especially Segond fracture, tibial plateau, and tibial spine avulsion in skeletally immature patients)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Takes 5-10 minutes; results immediate. Performed before MRI in all acute knee injuries to exclude bony injury.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantitative measurement of side-to-side anterior tibial translation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Hand-held device applied across the knee; takes 5 minutes. Used in research and pre-operative assessment; difference >3 mm side to side suggests ACL deficiency.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of the ACL and associated structures when examination and MRI are equivocal
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under general or regional anaesthesia; usually combined with definitive reconstruction. Day-case procedure.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is individualized based on age, activity level, occupation, sport demands, time since injury, associated injuries, skeletal maturity, and patient goals. The KANON trial (Frobell et al. 2010, 2013) showed that for young active adults with acute ACL rupture, structured rehabilitation with the option of delayed reconstruction produced similar outcomes at 2 and 5 years compared with early reconstruction in those who progressed to surgery (about 50% of the rehabilitation group). Initial management focuses on controlling pain and swelling (rest, ice, compression, elevation, NSAIDs), restoring range of motion (especially full extension), and beginning early quadriceps and hamstring strengthening. Bracing is used in selected cases for symptomatic instability. Patients are stratified into copers (asymptomatic with rehabilitation) and non-copers (recurrent instability). Operative reconstruction is recommended for: young athletes wishing to return to pivoting sport; patients with concurrent meniscal repair indication; patients with multi-ligament injury; chronic instability with giving-way episodes; and skeletally immature patients with significant tear (using transphyseal or physeal-sparing techniques). Arthroscopic reconstruction uses an autograft (patellar tendon, hamstring tendon, or quadriceps tendon) or allograft, with the choice driven by patient anatomy, activity, age, and surgeon experience. The graft is fixed in anatomic femoral and tibial tunnels. Concurrent meniscal repair, meniscal allograft transplant, lateral extra-articular tenodesis (in revision or high-risk patients), or articular cartilage procedures may be performed. The STaR trial 2022 showed that lateral extra-articular tenodesis added to ACL reconstruction in young pivoting athletes reduced graft failure from 11% to 4% at 2 years. Post-operative rehabilitation is structured in phases: 0-2 weeks (pain control, regain extension, partial weight bearing), 2-6 weeks (full range of motion, gait normalization), 6-12 weeks (strengthening, balance, low-impact aerobic), 3-6 months (running, agility, sport-specific drills), and 6-12 months (return to cutting and pivoting). Return to sport requires meeting objective criteria: symmetric quadriceps strength (>90% limb symmetry index), single-leg hop tests >90% symmetry, dynamic stability tests, psychological readiness scales (ACL-RSI), and at least 9 months post-surgery (each month delayed before return reduces re-injury risk by 51% up to 9 months — Grindem et al. 2016). Non-surgical patients follow similar functional progression with avoidance of cutting and pivoting sport in non-copers.

medicalTreatments.name: Structured rehabilitation programme (pre-habilitation and post-injury)
medicalTreatments.description: Progressive physiotherapy programme covering range of motion, strength, neuromuscular control, balance, and sport-specific drills. Pre-operative rehabilitation reduces post-operative stiffness and improves outcomes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores knee function in approximately 50-60% of non-copers without surgery; essential before and after reconstruction

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesia (paracetamol 1 g 6-hourly, ibuprofen 400 mg 8-hourly)
medicalTreatments.description: Pain control in the acute injury and post-operative phases. Short courses of stronger analgesia for the first 48-72 hours after surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adequate pain control in 70-80% of patients without opioids beyond 3-5 days

medicalTreatments.name: Hinged knee brace
medicalTreatments.description: Used in acute injury for symptomatic instability and for selected patients with isolated tears not undergoing reconstruction. Worn during high-risk activities to limit excessive motion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest functional improvement in non-coper rehabilitation; no proven prevention of secondary injury

medicalTreatments.name: Neuromuscular prevention programmes (PEP, FIFA 11+) for at-risk athletes
medicalTreatments.description: Pre-season and ongoing dynamic warm-up programmes combining strength, balance, plyometrics, and landing technique training. Performed 2-3 times weekly.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces non-contact ACL injuries by 40-70% in adolescent female athletes (multiple RCTs)

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (selected cases)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in chronic ACL deficiency with persistent effusion and post-traumatic osteoarthritis. Provides 6-12 weeks of symptomatic relief.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in 60-70% of patients with established OA; no disease-modifying effect

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic ACL reconstruction with autograft (BPTB, hamstring, or quadriceps)
surgicalOptions.description: Reconstruction using the patient's own tendon graft through anatomic femoral and tibial tunnels. Day-case procedure under general or regional anaesthesia, 45-90 minutes. Concurrent meniscal repair or chondral procedures as needed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Return to pre-injury sport in 65-80% of athletes; graft failure 3-15% (higher in young female pivoting athletes)

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic ACL reconstruction with allograft
surgicalOptions.description: Uses donor tendon (Achilles, tibialis anterior, patellar tendon). Avoids donor-site morbidity but has higher re-rupture rate in young athletes; used selectively in older or multi-ligament patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable function to autograft in adults over 30; higher failure rate (15-25%) in young athletes

surgicalOptions.name: Lateral extra-articular tenodesis (LET) with ACL reconstruction
surgicalOptions.description: Augmentation procedure using the iliotibial band to add rotational stability. Performed alongside primary or revision ACL reconstruction in young pivoting athletes or high-risk patients (STaR trial 2022).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft failure reduced from 11% to 4% at 2 years in young athletes (STaR trial)

surgicalOptions.name: ACL primary repair (selected proximal tears)
surgicalOptions.description: Suture repair of a proximal ACL avulsion when the ligament tissue is preserved. Performed within 4-6 weeks of injury. Limited evidence base; selected for distinct injury pattern.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 70-85% return to sport in selected cohorts; revision rate up to 20%

surgicalOptions.name: Tibial spine avulsion fixation (paediatric)
surgicalOptions.description: Arthroscopic or open fixation of the bony fragment with screw or suture. Indicated for displaced tibial intercondylar eminence fractures in skeletally immature patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Excellent union and functional outcomes (>90%) with anatomic reduction

surgicalOptions.name: Meniscal repair or partial meniscectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Performed concurrently with ACL reconstruction. Repair preserves meniscus when tear pattern allows; partial meniscectomy is used for irreparable or degenerative tears.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Meniscal repair success 70-90% when combined with ACL reconstruction; reduces long-term OA risk

alternativeTreatments.name: Blood-flow restriction (BFR) training during rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Application of a tourniquet at low pressure during low-load exercise to accelerate strength and muscle hypertrophy. Used in early post-operative rehabilitation when joint loading is restricted.

alternativeTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection
alternativeTreatments.description: Injection of concentrated autologous platelets and growth factors into the graft or joint. Evidence for accelerating ACL graft healing is limited; routine use is not recommended outside clinical trials.

whySeeSpecialist: A sports medicine physician or orthopaedic surgeon assesses the injury, identifies associated injuries, advises on surgical versus non-surgical management, and oversees rehabilitation. Specialist physiotherapy is essential for return to sport; coordination between surgeon, physiotherapist, and athletic trainer determines outcomes.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a surgeon performing at least 50 ACL reconstructions per year with documented outcomes, access to all graft choices, experience with concomitant meniscal repair, and partnership with experienced sports physiotherapy. Look for centres offering objective return-to-sport testing.
hospitalCriteria: MRI capability within 7 days of injury
keyFacilities: Paediatric orthopaedics for skeletally immature patients
Knee biomechanics laboratories for return-to-play testing
preventionStrategies: Use validated neuromuscular training programmes (PEP, FIFA 11+, KIPP) 2-3 times weekly during pre-season and in-season.
Train safe landing technique (soft landing with knees over toes, knees bent and hip-width apart).
Strengthen the hip abductors, gluteal muscles, and hamstrings to reduce dynamic valgus.
Implement gradual training-load progression to avoid sudden spikes that raise injury risk.
Match footwear to playing surface to optimize friction without excess grip.
Wear appropriate functional knee bracing in selected high-risk return-to-sport situations under specialist advice.
Educate athletes and coaches about ACL injury risk factors and warning signs of overload.
Maintain optimal sleep, hydration, and recovery to support neuromuscular performance.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere to the rehabilitation programme with regular physiotherapy sessions.
Maintain a healthy weight (BMI 20-25) to reduce knee joint loading.
Stop smoking; nicotine impairs ligament and graft healing.
Limit alcohol during the first 6 weeks after surgery.
Eat a Mediterranean-style diet rich in protein, oily fish, and antioxidants to support tissue healing.
Use ice and elevation in the first 72 hours after injury or surgery.
Avoid pivoting and cutting sports during rehabilitation until cleared by the specialist.
Plan return to sport with objective testing (limb symmetry index, hop tests, psychological readiness) at 9-12 months.

recommended: Adequate protein intake (1.2-2.0 g/kg/day) to support graft and muscle healing
Mediterranean-style diet rich in fruit, vegetables, oily fish, olive oil, and whole grains
Calcium 1,000-1,200 mg/day and vitamin D 800-1,000 IU/day for bone health
Hydration with 2-3 L of water daily during rehabilitation
avoid: Excess alcohol during early healing (impairs collagen synthesis)
Smoking and nicotine in any form (delays graft healing)
Excessive sugar and processed foods that promote weight gain during rehabilitation
Inadequate calorie intake when training volume increases

exerciseGuidelines: Follow a graded rehabilitation programme: gentle range of motion and isometric exercises in weeks 0-2; closed-chain strengthening at 2-6 weeks; running at 12-16 weeks; agility and cutting drills at 4-6 months; return to pivoting sport at 9-12 months after meeting objective criteria. Cycling and pool walking are useful adjuncts during early rehabilitation.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed physiotherapy exercises daily, twice daily in the early post-operative period.
Use a cold-therapy unit for 20-30 minutes 3-4 times daily for the first 7-10 days after surgery.
Elevate the leg above heart level when resting to reduce swelling.
Wear thromboprophylaxis stockings or take aspirin as prescribed after surgery.
Use crutches with prescribed weight bearing until the gait is normalized.
Take prescribed analgesia at scheduled intervals for the first 72 hours, then on demand.
Track quadriceps strength, range of motion, and pain in a rehabilitation diary.
prognosis: With appropriate management, 80-90% of athletes return to some form of sport at 9-12 months after reconstruction. Return to pre-injury level of pivoting sport is achieved by 65-80% in adults, and somewhat lower (~55-65%) in adolescent female athletes. Graft failure occurs in 3-15% overall, rising to 15-25% in young female pivoting athletes returning to sport before 9 months. Each additional month of rehabilitation before return reduces re-injury risk by 51% up to 9 months (Grindem et al. 2016). Long-term outcomes are dominated by post-traumatic osteoarthritis: 45-70% of patients develop radiographic OA at 10-20 years after ACL injury, irrespective of treatment, with higher rates after meniscectomy than meniscal repair or preservation. Symptomatic OA affects approximately 30% at 10-15 years. Quality of life is generally good but knee-related quality of life scores remain slightly below uninjured peers. Functional and psychological readiness (ACL-RSI) influence return to sport; fear of re-injury is the leading reason for not returning to pre-injury level. Long-term joint preservation depends on weight management, neuromuscular training, and meniscal preservation.
recoveryTimeline: Range of motion is regained over 4-6 weeks. Walking without crutches by 2-4 weeks. Stationary cycling and pool exercise by 6 weeks. Jogging at 12-16 weeks. Sport-specific drills at 4-6 months. Return to cutting and pivoting sport at 9-12 months with objective testing. Full graft remodelling continues for 12-24 months after surgery.
complications: Re-tear or graft failure (3-15%) — higher in young pivoting athletes returning early; revision reconstruction is more complex.
Post-traumatic osteoarthritis (45-70% at 10-20 years) — minimized by meniscal preservation, weight management, and neuromuscular training.
Knee stiffness or arthrofibrosis (1-5%) — managed with aggressive rehabilitation; rarely requires arthroscopic release.
Donor-site morbidity (anterior knee pain after BPTB graft, hamstring weakness after hamstring graft).
Deep vein thrombosis (1-3%) — prevented with early mobilisation and selected use of mechanical or pharmacological prophylaxis.
Surgical site infection (under 1% for arthroscopic reconstruction).
Persistent rotational instability requiring revision reconstruction or lateral extra-articular tenodesis.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — ACL Injury
supportResources.description: Patient-friendly resource from the AAOS covering ACL injury diagnosis, treatment, and rehabilitation.

supportResources.name: British Association of Sport and Exercise Medicine (BASEM)
supportResources.description: UK sports medicine professional body with patient information and clinician resources on ACL injury.

supportResources.name: Australian ACL Register
supportResources.description: Patient and clinician resource collecting data and outcomes from ACL reconstructions in Australia.

supportResources.name: FIFA 11+ Prevention Programme
supportResources.description: Evidence-based dynamic warm-up programme for football and team sports proven to reduce ACL and other lower-limb injuries.

confusedWithConditions.name: Knee Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: OA develops gradually over years with pain on weight bearing, crepitus, and joint-space narrowing on X-ray. ACL tear is an acute injury with audible pop, immediate effusion, and giving-way. ACL deficiency accelerates OA, so the two coexist long-term.

confusedWithConditions.name: Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Patellofemoral pain causes anterior knee pain aggravated by stairs and prolonged sitting, with no instability or effusion. ACL tear produces lateral instability with giving-way, audible pop, and large effusion after injury.

confusedWithConditions.name: Inflammatory Arthritis of the Knee
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Inflammatory arthritis produces bilateral symmetrical joint swelling, morning stiffness, and raised inflammatory markers. ACL tear is unilateral, post-traumatic, and shows ligament discontinuity on MRI without systemic features.

confusedWithConditions.name: Other knee ligament injuries (MCL, PCL, PLC)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Each knee ligament injury has a specific mechanism and examination pattern: MCL tear shows valgus laxity at 30° flexion; PCL injury shows posterior sag and posterior drawer; posterolateral corner injury shows dial test asymmetry. ACL tear features positive Lachman and pivot shift.

relatedConditions.name: Knee Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: ACL injury accelerates the development of knee osteoarthritis; meniscal preservation and neuromuscular training reduce long-term OA risk.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Inflammatory arthritis of the knee enters the differential for chronic swelling without trauma history.

relatedConditions.name: Achilles tendinitis (sports injuries)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are common sports injuries managed by sports medicine teams; tendinitis and ligament injuries coexist in some patients.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic pain syndromes
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-traumatic chronic pain may develop after ACL injury and requires multidisciplinary care.

faqs.question: What is an ACL tear?
faqs.answer: An ACL tear is a partial or complete rupture of the anterior cruciate ligament, the main stabilizer of the knee against forward and rotational movement. It typically occurs during pivoting, cutting, or landing in sport and produces an audible pop, rapid swelling, and a sense of the knee giving way.

faqs.question: How do you know if you have torn your ACL?
faqs.answer: Typical features are an audible pop at the moment of injury, immediate giving-way, severe pain, rapid knee swelling within hours, and inability to continue play. A positive Lachman test on examination and MRI confirming ligament discontinuity are the gold standards for diagnosis.

faqs.question: Can an ACL tear heal without surgery?
faqs.answer: Complete ACL tears do not heal back to their original strength because the ligament has poor blood supply. Some patients (about 50% of active adults in the KANON trial) function well with rehabilitation alone, avoiding pivoting sport. Surgery is recommended for those wishing to return to cutting sports or with recurrent instability.

faqs.question: What is ACL reconstruction surgery?
faqs.answer: ACL reconstruction is an arthroscopic procedure that replaces the torn ligament with a tendon graft (patellar tendon, hamstring, quadriceps, or donor) anchored in anatomic femoral and tibial tunnels. It is performed as day surgery under general or regional anaesthesia and takes 45-90 minutes.

faqs.question: How long is recovery from ACL surgery?
faqs.answer: Walking with crutches starts immediately; full weight bearing within 2-4 weeks. Stationary cycling at 6 weeks, jogging at 12-16 weeks, sport-specific drills at 4-6 months, and return to cutting and pivoting sport at 9-12 months with objective testing. Full graft remodelling continues 12-24 months.

faqs.question: When can I return to sport after ACL reconstruction?
faqs.answer: Return to pivoting sport is recommended at 9-12 months minimum and only after meeting objective criteria: quadriceps strength symmetry >90%, hop test symmetry >90%, dynamic stability, and psychological readiness. Each month delayed up to 9 months reduces re-injury risk by 51% (Grindem et al. 2016).

faqs.question: Which ACL graft is best?
faqs.answer: Bone-patellar tendon-bone, hamstring, and quadriceps autografts have similar long-term outcomes; choice depends on patient anatomy, sport, age, and surgeon experience. Allografts have higher re-rupture rates in young athletes. The 2022 STaR trial showed adding lateral extra-articular tenodesis reduces graft failure in young pivoting athletes.

faqs.question: Is the Lachman test painful?
faqs.answer: The Lachman test is generally not painful when performed correctly. The examiner gently pulls the tibia forward with the knee flexed 20-30 degrees and feels for the firmness of the endpoint and amount of translation. Guarding from pain or muscle spasm can limit the test in the acute setting.

faqs.question: Can children get ACL tears?
faqs.answer: Yes. ACL tears in children are increasing with youth sport participation. Skeletally immature patients (with open growth plates) require specialized physeal-sparing or transphyseal reconstruction techniques to avoid growth disturbance. Tibial spine avulsion is more common than mid-substance tear in younger children.

faqs.question: Will I get arthritis after ACL injury?
faqs.answer: Approximately 45-70% of patients develop radiographic knee osteoarthritis 10-20 years after ACL injury, regardless of treatment. Symptomatic OA affects about 30%. Meniscal preservation, weight management, neuromuscular training, and avoidance of repeated injury reduce long-term OA risk.

faqs.question: Why are women more at risk of ACL injury?
faqs.answer: Female athletes have 2-8 times higher ACL injury rates than men in the same sports because of anatomical factors (wider pelvis, narrower notch, smaller ACL), hormonal influences, and neuromuscular patterns (quadriceps-dominant landing with dynamic valgus). Neuromuscular training reduces this risk by 40-70%.

faqs.question: Can an ACL tear be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes, partly. Neuromuscular training programmes (FIFA 11+, PEP) reduce non-contact ACL injuries by 40-70% in adolescent female athletes. Strengthening the hip and core, safe landing technique, gradual load progression, and appropriate footwear lower individual risk.

faqs.question: How is an ACL tear diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is based on mechanism of injury, examination (positive Lachman and pivot shift tests, rapid effusion), and confirmatory MRI. Plain X-rays exclude fracture, especially Segond fracture and tibial spine avulsion in children. Diagnostic arthroscopy is reserved for equivocal cases.

faqs.question: What is a Segond fracture?
faqs.answer: A Segond fracture is a small avulsion fracture of the lateral tibial plateau where the lateral capsular ligament inserts. It is visible on plain X-ray and is pathognomonic for ACL injury, with 75-100% specificity. Identifying it on imaging should prompt MRI and ACL assessment.

faqs.question: What is the unhappy triad?
faqs.answer: The unhappy triad describes simultaneous injury to the ACL, the medial collateral ligament, and the medial meniscus, classically from a lateral blow to the knee in football or rugby. Modern MRI shows that the lateral meniscus is actually more commonly injured than the medial in true unhappy triads.

faqs.question: Can I do rehabilitation at home?
faqs.answer: A structured supervised programme guided by a sports physiotherapist gives the best outcomes. Home exercises are part of every phase. Tele-rehabilitation and app-based programmes are useful adjuncts but should be reviewed regularly by a clinician to progress safely.

faqs.question: What happens if you ignore a torn ACL?
faqs.answer: Untreated complete ACL tear leads to recurrent giving-way episodes, secondary meniscal and cartilage damage, and accelerated post-traumatic osteoarthritis. Pivoting sports become unsafe. Some patients function well in low-demand activities; assessment of functional needs guides management.

faqs.question: Is ACL reconstruction covered by insurance?
faqs.answer: Most insurers cover ACL reconstruction when functional instability is documented and conservative measures have been considered. Pre-authorization, imaging confirmation, and an orthopaedic specialist consultation are usually required. Coverage varies by plan and country.

faqs.question: Can I tear the ACL again after surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Graft failure occurs in 3-15% of patients overall and is highest (15-25%) in young female pivoting athletes returning to sport before 9 months. Contralateral ACL tear rates are also elevated. Adherence to rehabilitation, neuromuscular training, and delayed return to sport reduce risk.

faqs.question: What is lateral extra-articular tenodesis?
faqs.answer: Lateral extra-articular tenodesis (LET) is an augmentation procedure using a strip of iliotibial band to add rotational stability to ACL reconstruction. The 2022 STaR trial showed adding LET to ACL reconstruction reduced graft failure from 11% to 4% at 2 years in young pivoting athletes.

faqs.question: Should I have ACL surgery if I am over 40?
faqs.answer: Age alone is not a contraindication. ACL reconstruction is offered to active patients of any age with functional instability or those wishing to return to pivoting sport. Outcomes in patients over 40 are good when expectations and activity goals are appropriate. Coexisting osteoarthritis influences the decision.

metaTitle: ACL Tear: Symptoms, Diagnosis & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: ACL tear: pop, knee swelling, Lachman test, MRI, reconstruction surgery, and return to sport at 9-12 months. Medically reviewed sports medicine guide.
